ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice as a combined dish. Mapped to broader 'potato dish' (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible anchor. Jeera rice component not separately anchored in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Rice-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category for this combined potato curry and cumin rice dish. No specific FoodEx2 term exists for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice or sufficiently close prepared dish equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of jeera rice and 150 g of dum aloo curry, as typically served in a Singaporean Indian restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Dum Aloo With Jeera Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Dum Aloo With Jeera Rice' (cr_id G00010522). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice and boiled potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice as a combined dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on a weighted blend of: (1) basmati rice GI ~58 (University of Sydney GI Database, exact match), contributing approximately 57% of carbohydrate load; and (2) boiled/pressure-cooked potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database, exact match), contributing approximately 43% of carbohydrate load. Weighted average: (0.57 × 58) + (0.43 × 78) ≈ 33.1 + 33.5 = 66.6, rounded to 65 to account for yogurt and spice matrix moderating effect. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (45.3 g from 350 g × 25.19 g/100g) / 100 = 65 × 45.3 / 100 ≈ 29.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; ghee and added salt in preparation elevate sodium further
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and ghee in preparation; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (1.41:1) provides partial DASH-diet benefit, but a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, which represents 35–40% of the recommended daily limit and warrants portion control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Dual starch load (potato + basmati rice) produces a medium-to-high glycemic load of approximately 29.5 per serving, with carbohydrate at 25.19 g/100g and dietary fibre providing limited glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, substitute cauliflower rice for jeera rice where possible, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycaemic load; the yogurt matrix provides modest protein buffering but is insufficient to offset the dual starch contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 0 mg/100g; fat is moderate at 4.21 g/100g; however, ghee used in preparation contributes saturated fat not fully captured in the HPB composite value, and sodium at 231 mg/100g is a secondary cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal ghee or substitute with a small amount of unsaturated oil, monitor sodium intake given the 231 mg/100g baseline, and note that the iron content (2.55 mg/100g) and potassium (327 mg/100g) are cardioprotective micronutrients present in meaningful quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potatoes, tomato, and kashmiri red chili are nightshade vegetables containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who have nightshade sensitivity should avoid this dish; if consumed, request baked or boiled potato preparation to minimise AGE formation, and confirm that turmeric and ginger are used in material quantities for their anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dum Aloo with Jeera Rice is a North Indian vegetarian dish widely served in Singaporean Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It consists of two components: dum aloo, a slow-cooked spiced potato curry, and jeera rice, steamed basmati rice tempered with cumin seeds and ghee. The dum aloo is prepared by pressure-cooking or pan-frying baby potatoes and then slow-cooking them in a rich masala of yogurt, tomato, onion, kashmiri red chili, turmeric, ginger, garlic, garam masala, and coriander powder. The 'dum' technique involves sealing the pot and cooking over low heat to allow the potatoes to absorb the spiced gravy fully. Jeera rice is made by tempering cooked basmati rice with whole cumin seeds in ghee or oil, producing a fragrant, lightly flavoured rice. The combined dish has a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour profile with a creamy, tangy gravy from the yogurt base. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The dual starch composition (potato plus rice) creates a medium glycaemic index of approximately 65 and a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~29.5), making portion control important for diabetic patients. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, but contains dairy from yogurt and ghee. It is a culturally significant dish in the North Indian and Kashmiri culinary tradition and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore.

ingredient_synonyms.kashmiri chili: mild red chili with deep colour
ingredient_synonyms.tadka: tempering of spices in hot fat

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010522) with a reported confidence of 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database approved sources for basmati rice and boiled potato, clearly documented. Multilingual names are independently verified for this well-known North Indian dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010522) and is not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent combined dum aloo with jeera rice preparation as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no equivalent approved-source composite record was identified. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by the HPB source; the dish is dairy-containing (yogurt, ghee) but the composite cholesterol is reported as 0 mg/100g in the HPB record — this is plausible given the small quantities of ghee and yogurt relative to the potato and rice bulk. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (6.5), fat_g (4.21), and carbohydrate_g (25.19) are all non-zero. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this combined dish is not available in any approved source as of 2026. The input health_suitability entries referenced sugar as 2.0 g/100g and fibre as 2.5 g/100g in their reasoning text, but the actual HPB per_100g values are sugar 0.21 g/100g and dietary_fibre 3.7 g/100g; the health_context guidance in this record uses the correct HPB values.
